metadata.career_relevance: REST controllers are the public face of every Spring Boot backend. When Flipkart's mobile app loads your order history, a @GetMapping handles that. When you place an order on Razorpay, a @PostMapping processes it. Knowing @RestController, ResponseEntity, @PathVariable, @RequestBody, and correct HTTP status codes is required knowledge for every Java backend interview and every Spring Boot job.

hook.what_you_will_build: By the end of this topic, you'll build a complete CRUD REST API: @RestController with all four HTTP methods, @PathVariable for URL segments, @RequestParam for query strings, @RequestBody for JSON input, ResponseEntity for status control, and correct HTTP status codes (200, 201, 204, 404).

visual_aid.description: HTTP request/response flow through Spring Boot: (1) HTTP Request arrives → DispatcherServlet → @RestController method matched. (2) Input: URL path → @PathVariable extracted; query string → @RequestParam extracted; JSON body → Jackson deserializes to @RequestBody. (3) Method executes, calls service. (4) Output: return value serialized to JSON by Jackson; ResponseEntity wraps with status code and headers. (5) HTTP Response sent. Second panel: URL pattern table: GET /api/products (getAll), GET /api/products/{id} (getById), POST /api/products (create), PUT /api/products/{id} (update), DELETE /api/products/{id} (delete) — each with status code and description.
visual_aid.alt_text: HTTP request routing to @RestController, @PathVariable/@RequestParam/@RequestBody extraction, ResponseEntity status codes, CRUD URL pattern table

worked_example.scenario: Build a complete CRUD REST API for a product catalog. All five operations: list, get one, create, update, delete. Uses ResponseEntity for status codes. In-memory storage (no database yet).
worked_example.problem_statement:
Build `ProductController` with:
- GET /api/products → 200 + list
- GET /api/products/{id} → 200 + product or 404
- GET /api/products?category=X → filtered list
- POST /api/products → 201 + created product
- PUT /api/products/{id} → 200 + updated or 404
- DELETE /api/products/{id} → 204 or 404
worked_example.expected_output:
# Run the app, then test with curl:

curl http://localhost:8080/api/products
→ []  (200 empty list)

curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/products \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"name":"Wireless Mouse","category":"Electronics","price":799}'
→ {"id":"P001","name":"Wireless Mouse","category":"Electronics","price":799.0}  (201)

curl http://localhost:8080/api/products/P001
→ {"id":"P001","name":"Wireless Mouse","category":"Electronics","price":799.0}  (200)

curl http://localhost:8080/api/products/P999
→ (empty body, 404)

curl -X DELETE http://localhost:8080/api/products/P001
→ (empty body, 204)
things_to_notice: @RestController combines @Controller + @ResponseBody. Without @ResponseBody, Spring would look for a view template named 'Wireless Mouse' — wrong! @ResponseBody tells Spring: serialize the return value to the HTTP response body as JSON.

`@RequestParam(required = false) String category` makes the query param optional. If absent: null. If present: the value. This lets `GET /api/products` return all and `GET /api/products?category=Electronics` return filtered — same endpoint.
The Optional-to-ResponseEntity pattern: `findById(id).map(ResponseEntity::ok).orElse(ResponseEntity.notFound().build())` — this is idiomatic Spring. Every GET-by-id should use this pattern.
DELETE returns 204 No Content (not 200) on success. 204 means 'it worked, there's nothing to return'. Never return the deleted object — it no longer exists.

POST returns 201 Created (not 200 OK). 201 means a new resource was created. The response body contains the created resource with its server-assigned ID. Following REST conventions matters for interoperability.

The ProductRequest record separates input from output. Clients cannot set the id field (server assigns it), preventing over-posting attacks. This is the DTO (Data Transfer Object) pattern — critical for production security.

industry_examples.use_case: `GET /api/v3/products/{productId}` — @GetMapping, @PathVariable. `POST /api/v3/cart/items` — @PostMapping, @RequestBody with item details. `DELETE /api/v3/cart/items/{itemId}` — @DeleteMapping, 204 on success. `GET /api/v3/products?category=Electronics&sort=price&limit=24` — @RequestParam with multiple optional params. All return JSON, all use ResponseEntity for status codes.

`POST /v1/orders` — creates a payment order (@RequestBody with amount, currency, receipt). Returns 201 with orderId. `GET /v1/orders/{id}` — fetch order status (@PathVariable). `POST /v1/payments/{id}/capture` — capture a payment (@PathVariable + @RequestBody with amount). All authenticated. Follows the same @RestController patterns, just with auth middleware added.

`GET /api/portfolio/positions` — get all current positions. `GET /api/orders?status=PENDING` — filter by query param. `POST /api/orders` — place an order. `DELETE /api/orders/{orderId}` — cancel pending order (204 if cancelled, 400 if already executed). These are the same patterns you just learned.

applications.interview_relevance:
REST controller interview questions — must know:

- **'What is the difference between @Controller and @RestController?'** → @RestController = @Controller + @ResponseBody. Without @ResponseBody, return values are view names, not JSON.
- **'What status code should a POST that creates a resource return?'** → 201 Created, not 200 OK.
- **'What is the difference between @PathVariable and @RequestParam?'** → @PathVariable: part of the URL path (/products/{id}). @RequestParam: query string (/products?category=X).
- **'How do you return 404 for a missing resource?'** → `Optional.map(ResponseEntity::ok).orElse(ResponseEntity.notFound().build())`
applications.real_world_workflow:
Daily REST controller workflow:

- New resource: create record + controller + service + tests
- GET all: @GetMapping, return list, 200 implicit
- GET by id: @GetMapping/{id}, Optional → ResponseEntity
- POST: @PostMapping, @RequestBody, 201
- PUT: @PutMapping/{id}, Optional → ResponseEntity
- DELETE: @DeleteMapping/{id}, boolean → 204 or 404
- Test with MockMvc (@WebMvcTest) or curl

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Returning `null` from a method instead of ResponseEntity.notFound() — Spring returns 200 OK with empty body.
common_gaps.remediation: Never return null from a controller method. Use `Optional<T>` in the service and convert to ResponseEntity: `optional.map(ResponseEntity::ok).orElse(ResponseEntity.notFound().build())`. A client receiving 200 OK with an empty body is confused — they expect 404.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: curl returns HTTP 200 with empty body instead of 404 for missing resource

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: POST request returns 415 Unsupported Media Type — forgot to add `Content-Type: application/json` header.
common_gaps.remediation: Every POST/PUT with a JSON body requires `Content-Type: application/json` in the request. Spring won't know to parse the body as JSON without it. Always test with: `curl -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{...}'`
common_gaps.detection_pattern: 415 Unsupported Media Type when sending POST with JSON body

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Returning `ResponseEntity.ok(saved)` (200) for a POST — should be 201 Created.
common_gaps.remediation: POST that creates a resource should return 201 Created: `ResponseEntity.status(HttpStatus.CREATED).body(saved)`. This is a REST convention — clients and API gateways use status codes to determine caching behavior, retry logic, and monitoring.

key_takeaways: @RestController = @Controller + @ResponseBody: return values become JSON response body
@RequestMapping on class: base URL prefix for all methods
@RequestBody: deserialize JSON body to Java object
ResponseEntity<T>: control status code + body + headers
Status codes: 200 GET/PUT success, 201 POST create, 204 DELETE, 404 not found

self_check_questions.question: Write the controller method signature for: GET /api/orders/{userId}/items?status=PENDING&limit=10
Your GET /api/users/{id} endpoint returns 200 OK with an empty body when the user doesn't exist. How do you fix it?
What is the difference between `@RequestMapping("/api")` on the class and `@GetMapping("/products")` on a method?
self_check_questions.answer: @RequestMapping at the class level sets the base URL for ALL methods in that controller — every method URL is prefixed with it. @GetMapping at the method level adds the method-specific path segment. The final URL is: base + method path. So @RequestMapping('/api') + @GetMapping('/products') → `GET /api/products`. This lets you group related endpoints under a common prefix without repeating it on every method.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: **Spring Data JPA** — now that you can receive and return data via HTTP, Topic 13.3 connects to a real database. @Entity, @Repository extends JpaRepository, findById(), findAll(), save(), deleteById(). Spring Data generates the SQL for you. After Topic 13.3, your CRUD API uses a real H2 (or PostgreSQL) database instead of an in-memory Map.
